nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; adults are generally found in coastal waters, coral reef crevices, rocky outcrops, and seagrass beds at depths of 0–85 m.

diet.notes: A dedicated meaty-food predator. When first introduced, live saltwater feeder shrimp are generally advised to entice feeding. With patience, many individuals can be transitioned to frozen foods including silversides, krill, shrimp, and squid. Has been reported to consume smaller fish and crustaceans housed in the same aquarium.

summary: The Miles Lionfish (Pterois miles) is a venomous ambush predator from the Indo-Pacific, closely resembling the Volitan Lionfish. It tends to be nocturnal and generally adapts well to aquarium life, though its venomous spines demand careful handling.
careNotes: The Miles Lionfish is considered intermediate in difficulty and is generally reported to adapt well to aquarium conditions. It is primarily nocturnal — tending to hide in crevices during daylight and becoming more active during low-light periods or at feeding times. The dorsal, pelvic, and anal fins carry highly venomous spines; handlers should exercise extreme caution, and any sting should be treated by immersing the affected area in hot (but not scalding) water to denature the venom proteins. In most cases it will initially hide while acclimating and can later be found perched out in the open. Live feeder shrimp are generally advised for initial feeding, with patience required to transition to prepared/frozen foods. It has been known to consume smaller fish and any crustaceans small enough to fit in its mouth; tankmate selection should account for its predatory nature. Aquascape should combine open swimming areas with numerous caves, ledges, and overhangs to provide perching and resting spots. Strong filtration is advisable given that lionfish are messy eaters and heavy waste producers. The species is also an established invasive in the Mediterranean Sea and parts of the Atlantic; it cannot legally be shipped to Florida (US) due to invasive-species regulations. Temperature range averaged from multiple sources: LiveAquaria (72–78°F / 22–26°C), Reeflings (24–28°C), FishBase (24.5–29.3°C); averaged to 23–27°C. Tank minimum set at 500 L based on LiveAquaria's 120-gallon recommendation, biased larger per policy for active predators. Adult size averaged from four sources (LiveAquaria 38 cm, FishBase 43 cm, Wikipedia 35 cm, Reeflings 38 cm); range set to 20–38 cm for typical captive specimens. Reef safety is disputed: LiveAquaria rates it 'with caution' while Reeflings rates it 'no'; 'with caution' is used here but the species will actively prey on small fish and crustaceans — a reef-safe designation should be interpreted with extreme caution.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has been reported on rare occasions but is considered unlikely under typical home-aquarium conditions. Lionfish are broadcast spawners; females release buoyant mucus-wrapped egg masses that males fertilize in open water. Captive breeding attempts generally require species-dedicated systems, precise conditioning, and expert-level management.

fish.breedingNotes: Broadcast spawner; breeding in home aquariums is extremely rare and not reliably documented. Dedicated species systems with expert-level conditioning would be required.
